task: We serve a transformer model behind an inference layer. To push more requests per second we turned on dynamic request batching and raised the max batch size, and throughput on the dashboards did climb. But now during traffic spikes the inference worker abruptly dies mid-request and the supervisor restarts it. Right before every crash, the accelerator's device memory fills to 100% and the allocation for the layer activations fails partway through a forward pass; the exact same setup runs fine at low traffic, and adding a second worker didn't change anything. What's the actual root cause we need to fix?
rationale: Surface vocabulary (dynamic batching, raised max batch size, requests per second, throughput dashboards) points straight at inference-batching-throughput; 'worker dies and gets restarted' baits pod-oomkilled; 'fills up / need it to fit' baits model-quantization. But the ONE distinguishing detail is the a_signal verbatim: device memory hits 100% and the activation allocation fails during the forward pass. That is a VRAM capacity-exhaustion failure at compute time (gpu-oom-inference), not a scheduling/throughput problem, not a container memory-limit OOMKilled (it's GPU device memory during a forward pass), and not a size/accuracy tradeoff. Batching is in fact the trigger that pushed activations past VRAM, making the wrong answer maximally tempting.

task: Our GPU-backed model service is stuck at a low ceiling of requests per second and tail latency balloons whenever traffic climbs. On-call's first theory was that the card is memory-saturated and about to hit an out-of-memory crash, so one proposal was to shrink the model and another was to spin up more replicas to absorb the load. But nvidia-smi shows VRAM usage sitting around 20% and GPU compute utilization mostly idle, spiking only briefly per request — and even with a deep request backlog the server still processes one request at a time. Memory never runs out on this single box. How do we raise the serving rate?
rationale: Surface framing (GPU memory-saturated, 'about to hit an out-of-memory crash') baits gpu-oom-inference; 'shrink the model' baits model-quantization; 'spin up more replicas to absorb the load' baits hpa-autoscaling. The ONE distinguishing detail is the b_signal verbatim: VRAM at ~20% with GPU compute mostly idle while the server processes one request at a time under a full backlog — sufficient memory remaining and available compute, requests under-batched. That rules out gpu-oom (nothing is running out of memory), quantization (compute is idle, not memory/accuracy bound), and HPA (a single idle GPU means more replicas add idle capacity, not throughput). The fix is dynamic batching/queueing to pack the backlog.

task: Our checkout API fans a single user request out through a chain of five internal gRPC services (gateway -> orders -> pricing -> inventory -> tax). Everything rides on long-lived pooled HTTP/2 connections and we multiplex thousands of concurrent calls per connection. Last night the tax service's database went into a slow-query spiral and started taking 40-60s per response. From that moment, every checkout that reached that path just sat there: the gateway worker threads never returned, the requests didn't 500 and didn't error, they hung until the tax service recovered ~20 minutes later, and only then did the backlog drain. Two things we're certain of: (1) requests whose code path never touches the tax service completed totally normally the entire time, even ones sharing the exact same physical connections; and (2) there was zero packet loss or retransmission on any of those links - the network was clean, the downstream was simply slow. There is no upper bound on how long a caller will wait for a reply. How do we stop one slow service from freezing the whole request chain?
rationale: Root cause is the absence of a cascading timeout budget across nested RPC hops: a slow downstream makes callers hang indefinitely instead of failing fast (a_signal). The distinguishing details rule out every trap: unrelated requests on the SAME connection complete fine (kills http2-head-of-line, which would stall co-located streams regardless of destination) and the network is explicitly clean with no packet loss/retransmission (kills http2-head-of-line's transport-layer trigger). HTTP/2 multiplexing, pooled connections, and the five-hop fan-out are surface bait pointing at http2-head-of-line, tcp-connection-reuse, and distributed-tracing respectively, but only propagating deadlines through the chained RPC hops addresses the indefinite hang.

task: We run gRPC between our edge proxy and backend over a single shared HTTP/2 connection per proxy instance, with lots of small concurrent calls in flight. A few times an hour, everything on that connection freezes for 150-300ms all at once - including tiny health-check and cache-lookup calls that normally finish in under 2ms and depend on no slow service whatsoever. The freezes line up exactly with TCP retransmission spikes on that one link (we can see the packet loss in our NIC counters), and when the lost segment finally gets retransmitted, all the stalled calls unblock together in a single burst. We already set aggressive per-call timeouts and they make no difference - the calls aren't slow at the application layer, they're all just waiting on the same connection. Spreading the traffic across several connections (or dropping back to HTTP/1.1 with a connection pool) makes the stalls vanish entirely. What's going on and how do we fix it properly?
rationale: Root cause is transport-layer stream serialization: many concurrent streams on one physical TCP connection all stall together when a packet is lost/reordered, unblocking in a burst on retransmit (b_signal). Distinguishing details kill the traps: per-call timeouts already exist and don't help, and the frozen calls are sub-2ms with no slow downstream (kills grpc-deadline-propagation, which only manifests as an indefinite hang on a genuinely slow dependency); the freeze correlates precisely with TCP packet loss and spreading across MORE connections fixes it (kills tcp-connection-reuse, whose problem is too many short-lived connections/TIME_WAIT/port exhaustion - the opposite remedy). gRPC + timeouts, the single shared TCP connection, and the cross-service edge proxy are surface bait for grpc-deadline-propagation, tcp-connection-reuse, and distributed-tracing.

task: We run our order-processing service as six identical worker instances behind a queue. A few times an hour one worker's transaction is suddenly killed and rolled back by Postgres itself — the worker didn't crash, hang, or hit any timeout, the database returned an error and threw the transaction out on its own. It always happens when two workers are handling different orders at the same instant: one updates the `inventory` row and then the `ledger` row, while the other happens to touch those same two rows in the opposite order, and then Postgres aborts one of them, logging that each transaction was holding a row the other was waiting on. We assumed the fix is to put a Redis-backed lock in front of this code path so only one worker across the cluster can enter it at a time. Is that the right way to solve this?
rationale: Surface cues (six workers across a cluster, 'coordinate so only one enters at a time', Redis lock) pull hard toward distributed-lock-redis, while 'two workers touch the same two rows' baits optimistic-concurrency and 'transaction killed/waiting' baits connection-pooling. The one distinguishing detail is the a_signal: the database ENGINE itself aborts and rolls back one of two concurrent transactions that acquired the same two rows in opposite order (a circular wait the DB detects) — the canonical fix is consistent lock ordering + retry (database-deadlocks). A Redis lock would only mask it by serializing; it is not a lost-update overwrite (optimistic), and 'didn't hit any timeout' explicitly rules out pool exhaustion.

task: Our billing service is deployed as four auto-scaled replicas. Each replica has its own in-process scheduler that fires a 'send end-of-day summary email' job at 23:00. Ever since we scaled from one replica to four, every customer now receives the same summary email four times. The job almost entirely calls our email provider and a third-party invoicing API and barely reads or writes our own database. We tried wrapping the whole job in a transaction and taking row locks on a `jobs` table so the replicas would contend for it, but each replica still sends its emails and the database never reports any conflict or aborts anything. What we actually need is a guarantee that at 23:00 exactly one of the four replicas runs this job. How should we fix this?
rationale: Surface cues (wrapping in a transaction, taking row locks, replicas 'contend') bait database-deadlocks, 'the same job runs concurrently' baits optimistic-concurrency, and 'same email four times / duplicate sends' baits webhook-idempotency dedup. The distinguishing detail is the b_signal: four independent auto-scaled replicas each fire their OWN scheduler, the work hits external systems (email + invoicing API) and barely touches the DB, the database never raises a conflict or abort, and exactly one replica must run — cross-process mutual exclusion with no shared transaction semantics (distributed-lock-redis). 'DB never aborts anything' negates database-deadlocks; it is not a same-record lost update (optimistic); and it is a self-triggered scheduler on every replica, not an inbound provider retrying a webhook (idempotency).

task: Our product search keeps getting complaints. When a shopper types an exact model number like 'EX-4200', the manual for that exact product lands on the second page and people give up. Our stack runs two retrieval backends side by side — a keyword index and an embedding-based semantic index — and stitches their outputs into the single ranked list the user sees. Here's the confusing part: if I hit the keyword backend by itself, the EX-4200 manual comes back as the #2 hit; if I hit the semantic/vector backend by itself, it's in the top 8. Neither backend is 'missing' it — recall looks healthy on both. But in the final unified list the user actually sees, it sits around 15th, below a pile of loosely related accessories. We haven't touched either index lately; we did recently load a lot more embedding-heavy content. What do we fix so documents that BOTH backends already rank highly stop sinking in the combined results?
rationale: Surface vocabulary (embedding/semantic index, recall, vector backend, plus 'added embedding-heavy content') pulls hard toward vector-hnsw-tuning and embedding-model-selection, and the exact-model-number/keyword angle baits fulltext-bm25-tuning. The single distinguishing detail — querying EITHER index alone ranks the target near the top (recall is fine on both single indices), and it is ONLY the merged/unified list that buries it — matches hybrid-search-fusion's a_signal (the defect lives in how two independent retrieval streams are combined/ranked). Tuning HNSW recall, BM25 params, or swapping the embedding model cannot help because each index already returns the doc high; only the fusion of the two streams is broken.

task: Semantic search regression I can't pin down. Last week we shipped a change to make our similarity search faster — tail latency was killing us, so we turned down one of the index's query-time settings, and p99 dropped from ~380ms to ~95ms almost immediately. Right after that, users started reporting that documents they used to find by meaning (paraphrases, synonyms of the query) just don't come back at all anymore. My first instinct was our result-blending layer: we combine a text-match list and a semantic list and rerank them into one output, so I assumed the merge step was quietly dropping them. But when I bypass all of that and query the embedding index directly with the raw query vector, the documents that should be the closest matches simply aren't in the returned set — and they were there before last week's latency change. The exact-keyword side is completely unaffected. How do I get those near-matches back without throwing away the latency win we just got?
rationale: Surface vocabulary (a blending/rerank layer that combines a text-match list and a semantic list) baits hybrid-search-fusion as the obvious culprit, while 'semantic/embedding' dangles embedding-model-selection and the keyword mention dangles fulltext-bm25-tuning. The distinguishing detail — bypassing the blend and hitting the embedding index directly STILL omits the nearest matches, and the regression began exactly when a query-time setting was turned down, trading recall for a big p99 drop — matches vector-hnsw-tuning's b_signal (recall-vs-latency within a single ANN index, i.e. ef_search). Fusion is explicitly ruled out because the raw single-index query already misses them; nothing about the model or keyword analyzer changed, only the index's latency knob.

task: One of our downstream microservices (the pricing service) had a bad deploy and got slow — calls to it went from ~20ms to 60–90 seconds. During that window our whole checkout API fell over: requests came in and just sat there open for a minute or more before eventually returning, and the on-call graphs showed open connections climbing into the thousands with a big pile of sockets stuck in TIME_WAIT once traffic finally drained. First instinct was connection churn / socket handling on the edge. But when we dug in, every individual hop is healthy on its own and returns in milliseconds; the one thing that's actually wrong is that the moment that ONE pricing hop stalls at 90s, every service above it in the call chain blocks for the full 90s waiting on it — nobody upstream gives up early, so a single slow hop drags the entire request tree down and pins all our worker threads until it finally responds. We need the request tree to abort quickly when a hop is slow instead of every caller hanging for the full duration.
rationale: Surface words (open connections climbing into the thousands, sockets stuck in TIME_WAIT, 'connection churn') bait tcp-connection-reuse, and 'cascades through the service chain' baits http2-head-of-line and distributed-tracing. The distinguishing detail is the fail-SLOW behavior straight from a_signal: individual hops return in ms, but one slow hop makes every upstream caller block for its full duration and hang indefinitely — timeout constraints aren't cascading across RPC hops. That rules out tcp-connection-reuse (there the requests still complete within acceptable latency; here they hang and never fail fast), http2-head-of-line (that's multiplexed-stream HOL from packet loss on one connection, not one slow app-level hop starving the tree), and distributed-tracing (observability only — wouldn't stop the hang).

task: Our API gateway makes a burst of outbound HTTP calls to an internal auth service on every user request. Under peak load the gateway host starts throwing 'cannot assign requested address' and a chunk of outbound calls fail. Because they're failing under load, the team first assumed the auth service was too slow and wanted to add tighter timeouts on those calls. But the latency is fine — every individual auth call that DOES go through returns in 3–5ms, p99 is flat, and the auth service is nowhere near saturated. The real picture from `ss`/netstat: the gateway opens a brand-new socket for every single outbound call and tears it right back down, so tens of thousands of sockets pile up in TIME_WAIT and the box runs out of ephemeral ports. Nothing is hanging or timing out — the requests are fast; the host is just drowning in short-lived sockets and can't allocate new ones.
rationale: Surface words ('failing under load', 'wanted tighter timeouts', 'exhaustion') bait grpc-deadline-propagation and database-connection-pooling, and 'opens a new socket per call' brushes up against http2-head-of-line multiplexing. The distinguishing detail is straight from b_signal: individual requests complete within acceptable latency (3–5ms, p99 flat) yet ephemeral ports exhaust and TIME_WAIT sockets accumulate — connection lifecycle churn, not timeout semantics. That rules out grpc-deadline-propagation (nothing hangs or times out; latency is healthy), http2-head-of-line (this is per-call socket open/close, not many streams on one connection), and database-connection-pooling (it's OS-level outbound ephemeral ports/TCP sockets, not a DB connection pool).

task: For every release we keep two byte-for-byte identical copies of our whole production stack running side by side — same app servers, workers, queues, config, everything — and only one copy serves users at a time. Shipping is supposed to be a non-event: we deploy the new build onto the currently-idle copy, warm it up, and then tell the load balancer to send 100% of live traffic to it in one single flip. Last night that flip moved every user over at once and a bad setting broke checkout for the entire user base instantly; the handful of requests still mid-flight on the old copy also errored for about a second. We pointed the balancer straight back at the old copy and everyone recovered within seconds. Leadership now wants this release-and-fall-back process to be safer and feel less all-or-nothing. What approach should we standardize on?
rationale: The load-bearing detail is straight from the a_signal: TWO identical full production stacks, only one live at a time, and the load balancer flips 100% of traffic between them in a single cutover (rolling back by pointing the balancer back) — that is blue-green at the infrastructure layer. The surface words ('release safer', 'less all-or-nothing', 'roll back within seconds') are engineered to point at feature-flag-rollout, but there is no fractional/percentage exposure inside one environment — traffic is toggled wholesale between two whole environments, so feature-flag is wrong. dns-propagation-ttl is tempting because 'flip/cutover/point back' sounds like swapping a DNS record, but the switch happens at the load balancer between running environments, not via DNS TTL caching. pod-disruption-budget is baited by 'requests in flight errored for a second during the switch' (sounds like eviction fallout), but those errors come from the wholesale traffic flip, not from pods being drained/evicted during a node upgrade. No mechanism name is stated; only the observable two-environment/100%-toggle setup.

task: We're about to ship a rewritten pricing engine and product is nervous about blast radius. The ask: on day one it should be active for only about 5% of real users on our single running service, then we ramp it to 25% and eventually 100% over the week while we watch error and conversion rates. We are explicitly NOT standing up a second copy of the stack or adding any servers — it's the exact same processes running the whole time. If conversion tanks we need to turn the new engine off for everyone within seconds by changing one setting, with no redeploy and without re-pointing any load balancer or DNS. A couple of folks on the team keep calling this our 'zero-downtime cutover with instant rollback.' How should we implement it?
rationale: The load-bearing detail is straight from the b_signal: expose the change to ~5% of live users first, ramp through 25% to 100%, all inside ONE running service with no extra environment or servers, and kill it for everyone via a single setting with no re-routing — that is a percentage/flag rollout at the application-logic layer. The surface phrase 'zero-downtime cutover with instant rollback' is textbook blue-green vocabulary and is the primary bait, but there is deliberately NO second environment and NO traffic re-pointing, so blue-green-deploy is wrong. dns-propagation-ttl is baited by the explicit mention of 'without re-pointing DNS', yet nothing here involves DNS records or TTL caching. hpa-autoscaling is a softer lure via 'ramp' and 'watch load/error rates', but nothing is about spinning up more replicas on CPU/load. The distinguishing fraction-of-traffic-within-one-service detail makes exactly one skill correct.

task: Customers on our subscription plan are occasionally getting charged two or three times for a single monthly renewal, and several have received the same 'payment received' email 2-3 times within a couple of minutes of each other. Every one of these inbound POSTs to our public /webhooks/stripe endpoint carries a valid HMAC signature header that passes our verification check, and when we diff the raw request bodies they are byte-for-byte identical — same event id, same payload, same amount. Correlating the access logs, each burst of duplicates lines up exactly with windows where our endpoint was slow to respond (10-15s under load), and right after those slow responses the provider's delivery dashboard shows that event marked 'failed — retried'. We're worried either someone is forging or replaying signed events against our public URL, or our Stripe charge-creation call is somehow firing more than once per renewal. What's the actual root cause and what should we fix?
rationale: The distinguishing detail is a_signal: the SAME legitimate event id is delivered multiple times because the provider retried after our endpoint timed out (dashboard shows 'failed — retried'), producing duplicate charges/emails — the textbook case for an idempotency/dedup key on event id. Surface vocabulary is engineered to point at the traps: 'valid HMAC signature header that passes' and 'someone forging or replaying signed events' scream webhook-signature-verification, but the signature already passes and the events are genuine provider retries, so authenticity is not the defect. 'Charged two or three times' and 'our Stripe charge-creation call firing more than once' tempt payment-gateway-integration, but the bodies are byte-identical retries of one event, not a bug in charge wiring. 'Same email 2-3 times' / duplicate delivery brushes up against streaming-exactly-once (double-counting on restart), but this is HTTP webhook retry delivery, not a stream processor reprocessing a batch. Only idempotency is correct.

task: Our fulfillment service exposes a public endpoint, /webhooks/payments, and every inbound 'payment.succeeded' POST makes us mark an order paid and ship it. Over the last week we've had a rash of orders shipping out for free. In the logs, many of the offending requests look like duplicates — the same order's 'paid' event appears to arrive several times — so our first theory was that the payment provider was timing out and re-delivering events, and we started building a table to drop repeats. But when we reconciled the event ids and amounts against the provider's own dashboard, the vast majority were never issued by the provider at all: they come from IP addresses well outside the provider's documented ranges and reference orders and amounts that were never actually paid. In other words, anyone who learns the URL can POST a fabricated 'paid' event and we'll ship goods. We need inbound events that trigger fulfillment to be provably from our payment provider and nothing else. What's the actual root cause and fix?
rationale: The distinguishing detail is b_signal: the offending requests were never issued by the provider (absent from its dashboard, from foreign IPs, referencing unpaid orders) — they are forged/spoofed, and the stated requirement is that events be 'provably from our payment provider,' i.e. authenticate the sender. That is webhook-signature-verification (HMAC/secret-based origin authentication). Surface vocabulary is engineered to point at the traps: heavy emphasis on 'duplicates,' 'same event arrives several times,' 'provider timing out and re-delivering,' and 'a table to drop repeats' makes webhook-idempotency the obvious-but-wrong pick — but dedup cannot stop attacker-crafted events, which are not real provider retries. 'Fabricated POST / anyone can forge a request' tempts csrf-protection, but this is a server-to-server webhook with no browser session or cookies to ride, so SameSite/anti-CSRF tokens don't apply. 'payment.succeeded / mark order paid / amounts' tempts payment-gateway-integration, but the defect is unauthenticated inbound webhooks, not charge/refund wiring. Only signature verification is correct.

task: We pushed a homepage banner + price update about 20 minutes ago and it still hasn't reached customers. When I curl our origin/API directly it returns the NEW price, and the new value shows in our origin server access logs, so the backend is definitely serving fresh data. But customers keep reporting the OLD banner. It's not one person: users in Frankfurt, Sao Paulo, and Singapore, on Chrome, Safari, and mobile browsers, are all looking at the exact same old version at the same moment. One of our support reps cleared her browser cache and did a Ctrl+Shift+R hard refresh and it was still old for her; I opened the page in a brand-new incognito window on a different laptop on a different network and got the identical stale banner. The response carries Cache-Control: public, max-age=900. After roughly 15 minutes it finally flips to the new banner for everybody at once. What's actually going on and how do I make updates show up sooner?
rationale: Surface cues (Cache-Control: max-age, 'hard refresh', 'browser cache') bait http-client-caching; 'different regions' baits dns-propagation-ttl; 'still see old / stale' baits read-replica-lag. The decisive detail is the a_signal: EVERY user across regions/browsers/devices sees the identical stale content simultaneously, a colleague's browser-cache clear + hard refresh + a fresh incognito window on another network all have zero effect, and the origin itself verifiably returns fresh data. Identical global staleness that individual browser clears cannot touch means the staleness lives in a shared network-edge cache, not any single browser (rules out http-client-caching). Origin-verified-fresh rules out read-replica-lag/DB. It's one shared version, not per-resolver IP divergence, so not DNS TTL.

task: One specific customer keeps filing tickets that our new dashboard layout 'never shipped' for them, they still see last week's version with the old sidebar. Everyone else sees the new layout fine, including two other users in the same city and a colleague sitting right next to them on the same office Wi-Fi. The page is served through our CDN, so I first assumed something at the edge, but only this one account is affected. When I had them press Ctrl+Shift+R (hard refresh) it instantly rendered the new dashboard, and clearing their browser cache also fixed it immediately, but after they click around normally for a while it reverts to the old sidebar for them again. Nobody else can reproduce it no matter what I try. Where should I be looking?
rationale: Surface cues ('served through our CDN', 'something at the edge', stale-content vocabulary) bait cdn-cache-invalidation, and 'one client stuck on the old thing' loosely baits dns-propagation-ttl (per-client TTL caching). The decisive detail is the b_signal: exactly ONE user is affected while everyone else (same city, same office Wi-Fi) is fresh, and that user's OWN hard refresh (Ctrl+Shift+R) or browser cache clear resolves it instantly for them. A shared edge/CDN cache would make everyone in a region stale at once, not a single account others sitting beside them cannot reproduce, and a browser-level hard refresh clearing it points squarely at that individual browser's HTTP cache (rules out cdn-cache-invalidation). A stale DNS resolver would not be fixed by clearing the browser cache and would not flip back on in-app navigation, ruling out dns-propagation-ttl.

task: Bug report from our storefront team. After merchandising edits a product's title and price, the save clearly goes through, but shoppers keep seeing the OLD title and price for a good 10-20 minutes. It's not tied to any one account: every visitor, in every region, logged in or anonymous, sees the exact same outdated version at the same moment, which honestly makes it feel like our datastore is handing back old rows to everyone. But here's the thing that doesn't fit that theory -- when I curl our API host directly it already returns the new title and price instantly, every single time. Our platform team can push the change live everywhere immediately by clearing the shared layer that fronts the public site in each region; if they don't, it just corrects itself after a fixed interval. What's the root cause and how do we fix it?
rationale: Surface words (stale content after a write, 'feels like the datastore hands back old rows,' self-resolving staleness) point straight at read-replica-lag, the primary trap. The single distinguishing detail from a_signal flips it: querying the origin/API host directly always returns FRESH data, so the database/read path is fine -- the staleness is uniform across ALL users in ALL regions for many minutes and is cleared by a manual flush of a shared fronting layer. Replica lag is per-user (only the writer briefly misses their own write) and resolves in seconds, not minutes for everyone, so it's excluded. http-client-caching is tempting (stale served content, self-clears) but is per-browser and wouldn't be identical for every visitor at once, nor cleared by a server-side flush, and the API JSON returns fresh -- so it's out. dns-propagation-ttl shares the 'TTL / self-resolves after a fixed interval / regional' surface but is about resolving the wrong IP, not serving old page content while the host answers fresh -- excluded. Only cdn-cache-invalidation matches origin-fresh + globally-uniform-stale + purge/TTL resolution.

task: Support keeps filing the same complaint. A user updates their profile bio (or posts a comment), the request comes back 200 success, but the page that renders immediately after still shows their OLD bio. If they wait two or three seconds and refresh, it's correct. It only ever affects the person who just made the change -- nobody else sees stale data, and a given user only sees it on their own just-submitted edit, never on anyone else's content. It clears on its own within a few seconds and nobody flushes anything to fix it. It reads like some layer is serving a cached old copy right after the update. The part that rules that out for me: when I connect straight to the node that took the write and select that row, the new bio is already stored there. What's going on and how do we fix it?
rationale: Surface words (stale copy served right after an update, self-clears in a few seconds, 'some layer is serving a cached old copy') point at cdn-cache-invalidation, the primary trap. The distinguishing detail from b_signal flips it: the write SUCCEEDED and querying the write-side node directly confirms the new value is already stored, yet an immediate read returns the old value -- and it's strictly per-user, only on the user's own just-written data, resolving in seconds with no purge/flush. CDN staleness would be uniform across all users for minutes and need a purge, and the origin would answer fresh -- excluded because here it's the read path itself returning old and it's scoped to the writer. http-client-caching is tempting (old value shown right after save, clears shortly) but would be per-browser/cache-header driven, not fixed by simply hitting the DB, and the freshly-rendered next request returns old server data -- excluded. optimistic-concurrency-control fits 'I saved but see the old value' as a possible lost update, but the write is NOT lost -- the write node confirms the row exists -- so it's a read-visibility timing issue, not two writers overwriting; excluded. Only read-replica-lag matches write-persisted-on-primary + own-write-not-visible-immediately + seconds-to-converge.

task: Our product-catalog search (a single Elasticsearch text index) has a relevance problem the team keeps complaining about. When a shopper searches `running shoes`, the #1 result is our giant 'Size & Care Guide' — an 8,000-word page that happens to repeat 'running' and 'shoes' dozens of times — while the actual 'Trailblazer Running Shoe' product sits on page two. Separately, searching the plural `boots` returns nothing for items whose titles are written as `boot`, and a query for `the north face` gets swamped by matches on the ultra-common word `the`. Our staff engineer is lobbying hard to stand up a second, meaning-based retriever and blend its hits with the keyword hits to 'finally fix relevance,' and someone else insists our product vocabulary is just too niche for whatever model we'd pick. But today the entire stack is keyword-only — there are no embeddings or vector search anywhere in it — and every one of these misfires traces back to how the text index weights repeated words, penalizes long documents versus short ones, folds word variants like plural/singular, and handles ultra-frequent words. What should we actually fix?
rationale: Every symptom is a defect inside one inverted/lexical index: repeated-term over-weighting (term-frequency saturation, the k1 knob), long documents outranking focused ones (document-length normalization, the b knob), plural/singular non-matching (stemming/analyzer), and a stop-word being scored. The decisive distinguishing detail is that the whole stack is keyword-only — there is NO second retriever, embedding, or vector index in existence — so hybrid-search-fusion (the loud 'add a semantic retriever and blend the two lists' suggestion) has nothing to fuse, embedding-model-selection ('vocabulary too niche for the model') presupposes a semantic pipeline that doesn't exist, and vector-hnsw-tuning ('meaning-based retriever') is equally moot. Fixes live entirely in single-index lexical scoring and tokenization, which is exactly fulltext-bm25-tuning.

task: Our help-center search already runs two retrievers side by side: a classic keyword index and a semantic/vector index. Here's the maddening part — if I query either one on its own, the correct article shows up in its top three almost every time. Keyword-only: great. Vector-only: great. But the single ranked list we actually show users, which we build by merging the two systems' outputs, buries that same correct article down around rank 15. Digging in, the keyword engine emits relevance scores in the 0-40 range while the vector engine emits similarities between 0 and 1, and the way we stitch the two lists together lets the raw keyword numbers completely dominate, so the vector-only wins get flattened out of the top results. Half the team wants to retune the keyword scoring knobs because 'the keyword scores are obviously running too hot,' another wants to swap out the embedding model for our domain, and a third wants to widen the nearest-neighbor search so it pulls more candidates. What's actually broken here?
rationale: The decisive distinguishing detail is that each retriever, queried independently, already returns the correct article in its own top three — so no single index is malfunctioning. The failure appears ONLY when the two ranked lists are combined, and the cause is incompatible raw score scales (0-40 vs 0-1) letting one stream swamp the other, i.e. a merge/combination defect solved by rank-based fusion. That rules out the three tempting single-index fixes: fulltext-bm25-tuning ('retune keyword knobs') is moot because keyword-alone is fine, embedding-model-selection ('swap the model') is moot because vector-alone is fine, and vector-hnsw-tuning ('widen the neighbor search') is moot because there's no recall/latency miss in the vector stream — the right doc is already there. The problem is purely how the two heterogeneous streams are fused, which is hybrid-search-fusion.

task: Search over our help-center regressed right after last week's search-cluster reindex, and it 'feels dumber.' The first thing we suspected was the semantic side, so we spent a day adjusting how many candidate neighbors the approximate index scans per query and even re-embedded the whole corpus with a larger, higher-dimension model — zero change. The actual pattern turned out to be narrower: when a user types a word in its plural or past-tense form, an article that unmistakably covers the topic but only ever spells that word in its singular/base form stops appearing in results, yet searching the base form surfaces it instantly. On top of that, a low-value release-notes page that repeats one ordinary word dozens of times now outranks the one-paragraph canonical answer whose title is exactly that word. This ranking path is purely query-words-versus-document-words plus how often each word occurs in a document — there is no synonym or meaning matching involved at all. Which knob is actually wrong?
rationale: Surface words (approximate-index neighbor count, higher-dimension embedding model, 'search feels dumber') push hard toward the semantic/vector side. But the distinguishing details are pure token-level lexical statistics: (1) plural/past-tense query words fail to match the same literal word in singular/base form while the base form matches — analyzer/stemming behavior drift; (2) a page that repeats one common word many times outranks the exact-title answer — term-frequency weighting miscalibration. Both are a_signal ('token-level statistics'), so gold=fulltext-bm25-tuning. vector-hnsw-tuning is the primary trap (they even re-tuned the neighbor-scan breadth, no effect — it's not a recall/latency/missing-neighbor issue). embedding-model-selection is excluded ('re-embedded with a larger model — zero change'). hybrid-search-fusion is excluded because the failure is same-word inflection, not synonyms/meaning ('no synonym or meaning matching involved at all' — fusion fixes exactly what is stated to be irrelevant here).

task: We tuned our semantic (vector) search last sprint to shave p99 latency because the endpoint was timing out under load. Since then, users report that for certain queries a few obviously-relevant documents — sometimes the single best answer — silently drop out of the top results. The documents are definitely still there: fetching them by id works fine, and when we run a slow exhaustive brute-force similarity pass over the entire corpus offline, those exact items come back as the true closest matches, so the vectors and the embedding model themselves look correct. We initially blamed the keyword/text-matching configuration and the stop-word list, and we even debated whether we needed to blend keyword hits back in — but this code path never touches keywords, it's pure vector similarity. If we dial the per-query search breadth back up the dropped documents reappear, but the latency win evaporates. How do we recover completeness without giving back the latency?
rationale: Surface words (docs 'drop out of results', 'we blamed the keyword/text-matching config and stop-word list') tempt toward the lexical/keyword side. But the distinguishing detail is the recall-versus-latency tradeoff: after a change made to cut p99 latency, true nearest neighbors go missing from the approximate results, an exhaustive exact-similarity scan confirms those items ARE the closest matches (so vectors are fine and the approximate traversal is under-searching), and increasing per-query search breadth brings them back at the cost of latency. That is b_signal exactly ('recall-latency tradeoff degradation / missing neighbors diagnosed via recall/exact-similarity measurement'), so gold=vector-hnsw-tuning. fulltext-bm25-tuning is the primary trap but is explicitly excluded ('this code path never touches keywords, it's pure vector similarity'). embedding-model-selection is excluded because the brute-force exact scan returns the missing docs — the embeddings are correct, only the index traversal misses them. hybrid-search-fusion is excluded — there is a single retrieval method and blending keyword results was explicitly ruled out.

task: Our vision-model inference service runs on a pool of Kubernetes GPU nodes. During traffic spikes the serving containers keep dying and restarting, and clients get 5xx bursts every time a container recycles — it really looks like the platform is memory-recycling the pods. But the numbers don't add up: the container's resident memory (RSS) sits around 40% of the pod's configured memory limit right up to the moment it crashes, and the platform never records the pod as exceeding its limit and never evicts it. The actual crash is an allocation failure raised by the model runtime mid-forward-pass, when the accelerator's dedicated on-board memory fills up while host RAM stays fine. It only happens when a burst of large requests gets served together, and capping the per-request batch size makes the restarts stop completely. What's the root cause we should fix?
rationale: Surface cues (containers dying/restarting under load on Kubernetes GPU nodes, 5xx on every recycle, 'platform memory-recycling the pods') scream a container/cgroup OOM, pulling hard toward the primary trap pod-oomkilled; 'make the model use less memory' tempts model-quantization and the word 'batch' tempts inference-batching-throughput. The one distinguishing detail defeats all three: the container's RSS stays ~40% of its configured memory limit and the pod is never flagged as over-limit or evicted (so it is NOT a cgroup/pod OOM), the failure is an allocation in the accelerator's on-board (device) memory raised by the runtime mid-forward-pass, and capping the per-request batch size resolves it — exactly the gpu-oom-inference a_signal (device-memory OOM at inference, independent of container memory limits, fixed by batch-size reduction). Quantization is an accuracy-tradeoff shrink (fix here is batch size, not weights) and inference-batching-throughput is about raising throughput, not stopping crashes from oversized batches.

task: Our recommendation-model service runs on those same Kubernetes GPU nodes. After several hours of uptime — or partway through a long batch-scoring job — the serving containers get killed and restarted with exit code 137, and we eat 5xx during every restart. Since it's a GPU model workload, everyone assumed the accelerator was running out of memory. But nvidia-smi shows GPU memory utilization low and flat the entire time, with gigabytes of device memory free, and the model never fails a tensor allocation. What actually climbs is the container's resident system memory — heap plus page cache and buffers creep up steadily, and right before each kill the process's RSS reaches the container's configured memory limit, at which point the kernel's out-of-memory killer terminates it (hence the 137). Bumping the container's memory limit up and trimming the process's overall footprint stopped the restarts. What's the real root cause?
rationale: Surface cues (GPU nodes, an ML model workload, 'running out of memory') pull hard toward the primary trap gpu-oom-inference, while 'containers keep getting killed and restarted' tempts pod-disruption-budget (evictions during drains) and hpa-autoscaling (scale out under load). The one distinguishing detail defeats all three: GPU/device memory stays low, flat, and free with no failed tensor allocation, whereas the container's system RSS (heap + page cache + buffers) climbs to the container's configured memory limit and the kernel OOM-killer terminates the process (exit 137), fixed by raising the container memory limit / shrinking the footprint — exactly the pod-oomkilled b_signal (cgroup memory-limit enforcement across all system memory types, not CUDA device memory). It is not a node-drain eviction (PDB) and not a CPU/load scaling problem (HPA) — it's a slow memory-footprint climb hitting the container limit.
